content.text: Text generation is the most mature application domain of generative AI. Large language models (LLMs) trained on hundreds of billions of tokens can complete sentences, answer questions, summarise documents, translate between languages, and hold multi-turn conversations — all from the same model, using the same autoregressive next-token prediction objective. The shift from task-specific NLP models (a BERT for classification, a separate seq2seq for translation) to a single prompted LLM handling all tasks simultaneously is one of the defining transitions of the foundation model era.
Given a prefix, the model continues text. Underlying mechanism: sample from p(x_t | x_{<t}) token by token. Used in creative writing, story continuation, code completion.
Condense a long document into a short one. Abstractive summarisation (generate new phrasing) is a natural fit for LLMs; extractive summarisation (copy key spans) is a simpler baseline.
Prompt: "Translate to French: <text>" — the model continues with the translation. Modern LLMs approach supervised MT quality on high-resource language pairs without task-specific training.

Closed-book QA: the model answers from parametric memory. Open-book QA (retrieval-augmented generation, RAG): retrieved passages are prepended to the context, reducing hallucination by grounding answers in retrieved facts.

Multi-turn conversation encoded as a sequence of alternating user/assistant turns. The model sees the entire history and predicts the next assistant turn. Chat-tuned LLMs (via RLHF/DPO) are aligned to be helpful, harmless, and honest in this format.
Framed generatively: "Is this review positive or negative? Answer: " — the model generates a label token. This instruction-following framing replaces dedicated classifiers for many NLP tasks.
In practice, text generation is accessed via an API that wraps the autoregressive model. The core parameters controlling output quality and diversity are temperature, top-p, and max tokens.
Decoding Strategies and Their Effect on Output
The LLM outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each step; the decoding strategy determines how the next token is chosen from that distribution.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch import Tensor


def temperature_scale(logits: Tensor, temperature: float) -> Tensor:
    """
    Temperature T > 1 flattens the distribution (more random/diverse output).
    Temperature T < 1 sharpens it (more greedy/repetitive output).
    T = 1.0 leaves the model distribution unchanged.
    """
    return logits / temperature


def top_p_filter(logits: Tensor, p: float) -> Tensor:
    """
    Nucleus sampling (Holtzman et al. 2020): keep the smallest set of tokens
    whose cumulative probability mass exceeds p, zero out the rest.
    Dynamically truncates the tail of the distribution per step.
    """
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    sorted_probs, sorted_idx = probs.sort(descending=True)
    cumsum = sorted_probs.cumsum(dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens once cumulative probability exceeds p
    remove = cumsum - sorted_probs > p
    sorted_probs[remove] = 0.0
    # Scatter back to original vocab order
    probs = torch.zeros_like(probs).scatter_(0, sorted_idx, sorted_probs)
    return probs.log()   # return log-probs for consistency


def sample_token(
    logits: Tensor,
    temperature: float = 1.0,
    top_p: float = 0.9,
) -> int:
    """
    Apply temperature then nucleus sampling, then draw one token.
    Typical production values: temperature=0.7, top_p=0.9 for creative tasks;
    temperature=0.0 (greedy) for deterministic/factual tasks.
    """
    if temperature == 0.0:
        return int(logits.argmax().item())   # greedy
    scaled = temperature_scale(logits, temperature)
    filtered = top_p_filter(scaled, top_p)
    probs = F.softmax(filtered, dim=-1)
    return int(torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1).item())

Autoregressive models generate one token at a time. For long outputs, streaming delivers tokens to the user as they are produced rather than waiting for the full response. This dramatically improves perceived latency.

def stream_response(prompt: str) -> None:
    """Stream tokens to stdout as they are generated."""
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            print(text, end="", flush=True)
    print()   # newline after completion


# With async (FastAPI / async frameworks)
import anthropic

async def stream_to_client(prompt: str):
    """Async generator that yields text chunks for server-sent events."""
    async with anthropic.AsyncAnthropic().messages.stream(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            yield text
Exp of avg negative log-likelihood; lower = better fit to held-out text
Does not correlate well with downstream task quality
Penalises valid paraphrases; saturates on good models
Longest common subsequence recall vs reference
Reference quality dependent; abstractive outputs penalised
Cosine similarity of contextual embeddings vs reference
A stronger LLM scores outputs on rubric (helpfulness, accuracy, style)
Position and verbosity biases; judge may agree with itself

LLMs are trained to predict plausible continuations, not to retrieve verified facts. A model will confidently generate text that contradicts its training data or fabricates citations, figures, and names. This is called hallucination — a consequence of next-token prediction without a grounded knowledge store.
: prepend retrieved passages to the context so the model can answer from text rather than memory. Reduces hallucination but does not eliminate it.
: instruct the model to cite the specific passage from which each claim comes. Allows post-hoc verification.
: greedy decoding reduces random surface variation but does not fix hallucination; the model still generates its most probable (potentially wrong) token.

# Zero-shot: task described in the prompt, no examples
zero_shot = "Classify the sentiment of this review as positive, neutral, or negative:\n{text}"

# Few-shot: 2–3 labelled examples in the prompt
few_shot = """
Classify sentiment. Examples:
  Review: 'Amazing product!' -> positive
  Review: 'It broke after a week.' -> negative
  Review: 'Does the job, nothing special.' -> neutral

Review: '{text}' ->"""

# Chain-of-thought: instruct the model to reason step by step before answering
cot = """Solve the following problem. Think step by step before giving your final answer.

Problem: {problem}

Let's think step by step:"""

# Structured output: constrain the model to return JSON
structured = """
Extract the following fields from the email below and return valid JSON only:
{"sender": "...", "subject": "...", "action_items": [...]}

Email:\n{email}"""

Mental model: a text LLM is a universal document continuation engine. Given any prefix (prompt), it produces the statistically most likely completion under its training distribution. Every NLP task — classification, translation, QA, summarisation — can be reframed as a completion task by choosing the right prompt prefix. The model is not reasoning from first principles; it is finding the completion its training distribution assigns highest probability. Hallucination occurs when the most probable completion is a plausible-sounding but false sentence.

The shift from task-specific NLP models to prompted LLMs brings substantial benefits across the development lifecycle. In the pre-LLM era, building a system to handle summarisation, translation, QA, and sentiment analysis simultaneously required four separate models, four training datasets, and four inference pipelines. A single prompted LLM replaces this with one model and one inference server—a dramatic reduction in operational complexity.
From a capability standpoint, LLMs exhibit

that task-specific models cannot: show the model two or three examples of a new task format in the prompt and it adapts immediately, without retraining. This enables rapid prototyping of NLP features in hours rather than the weeks required to collect data, train, and evaluate a supervised model. In addition, LLMs encode broad world knowledge from training, enabling factual QA and reasoning tasks that would require specialised knowledge graphs or retrieval systems in earlier architectures.
Single model for all text tasks:
One LLM deployment handles generation, summarisation, translation, classification, extraction, and dialogue—replacing multiple specialised models.
New tasks are defined in the prompt; no retraining or labelled data required. GPT-4 achieves competitive few-shot performance on benchmarks that required full fine-tuning with earlier models.
Long context windows (128K–1M tokens) allow the model to reason over entire documents, codebases, or conversation histories within a single inference call.
Training on multilingual corpora gives LLMs strong translation and cross-lingual capabilities without separate language-specific models.
Token-by-token delivery makes 30-second generations feel fast by showing immediate progress—critical for user-facing applications.
JSON-mode and function-calling constrain outputs to valid schemas, enabling safe integration into downstream systems without brittle text parsing.
LLM-based text generation carries significant trade-offs that make it unsuitable as a direct replacement for specialised models in all cases. The most critical is

: because LLMs predict statistically plausible continuations rather than retrieving verified facts, they generate confident-sounding false statements. A model may fabricate citations, invent statistics, or describe historical events that did not occur—all with the same surface fluency as accurate text. RAG and grounding reduce but do not eliminate this failure mode.

Cost and latency are structural constraints. Autoregressive generation is sequential—each token requires one forward pass through the model, meaning generation time scales linearly with output length and is difficult to parallelise. A 100-billion-parameter model generating 500 tokens at 10ms per token takes 5 seconds per request, which is expensive at scale. Smaller distilled models (7B–13B parameters) generate faster but sacrifice capability, especially on complex reasoning tasks.

Next-token prediction optimises for plausibility, not factual accuracy. No prompt engineering fully eliminates hallucination; architectural solutions (RAG, tool use) are required for high-stakes factual tasks.

Despite growing context lengths, attention is O(n²) in sequence length. Very long contexts increase latency, cost, and the risk of the model losing focus on distant information (the lost-in-the-middle problem).
Proprietary LLM APIs charge per token; a summarisation pipeline processing millions of documents may cost orders of magnitude more than a fine-tuned BERT.
Temperature > 0 produces different outputs on identical inputs. Tasks requiring reproducibility (legal document analysis, audit trails) need temperature=0 plus version-pinning of the model.
Small changes in phrasing can dramatically change outputs. Prompt brittleness makes LLM pipelines harder to maintain than traditional rule-based or supervised systems.
LLMs inherit and amplify biases from training corpora—gender, racial, and cultural biases appear in generated text and require active mitigation.
When to Use LLMs for Text — and When Not To
Open-ended generation where quality varies across requests and a human reviews outputs (creative writing, drafting, brainstorming).
Multi-task pipelines where the same deployment must handle diverse text tasks without retraining.
Low-data NLP tasks where collecting training data for a supervised model is expensive or slow — LLM few-shot prompting is faster to deploy.
Document understanding (summarisation, extraction, QA) over unstructured text that does not fit clean supervised patterns.
Conversational agents where the system must handle unpredictable user intent across many turns.
High-volume, narrow classification tasks (spam detection, sentiment at billions-of-events scale) — a fine-tuned BERT is 100× cheaper and faster.
Tasks requiring guaranteed factual accuracy without hallucination risk — use RAG with citation verification, or a database query rather than an LLM.
Real-time tasks with strict latency SLAs under 100ms — specialised models or rule-based systems are more predictable.
Highly structured data extraction where a regex or a trained NER model is sufficient and cheaper.
Legally or regulatorily sensitive outputs requiring auditable, deterministic, fully explainable reasoning paths.
How to Implement — Complete Text Pipeline

A production text pipeline requires more than a single API call. It needs input validation, retry logic, cost tracking, output parsing, and evaluation. The following shows a complete pattern covering summarisation, structured extraction, and evaluation using the Anthropic API.

import anthropic
import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


@dataclass
class LLMCall:
    """Tracks one API call for cost and latency monitoring."""
    prompt_tokens: int = 0
    completion_tokens: int = 0
    latency_s: float = 0.0
    cost_usd: float = 0.0


def call_with_retry(
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens: int = 512,
    system: str = "",
    temperature: float = 0.0,
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> tuple[str, LLMCall]:
    """
    Call the Anthropic Messages API with exponential-backoff retry on
    rate-limit or server errors. Returns (text, metrics).
    """
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        try:
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            kwargs = dict(model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens,
                         messages=messages, temperature=temperature)
            if system:
                kwargs["system"] = system
            resp = client.messages.create(**kwargs)
            latency = time.perf_counter() - t0

            # Cost estimate (claude-sonnet-4-6 approximate rates)
            p_toks = resp.usage.input_tokens
            c_toks = resp.usage.output_tokens
            cost = p_toks * 3e-6 + c_toks * 15e-6  # per-token USD

            metrics = LLMCall(p_toks, c_toks, latency, cost)
            return resp.content[0].text, metrics

        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            if attempt < max_retries - 1:
                time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
            else:
                raise
        except anthropic.InternalServerError:
            if attempt < max_retries - 1:
                time.sleep(1)
            else:
                raise


def summarise_and_extract(document: str) -> dict:
    """
    Two-step pipeline: summarise the document, then extract structured metadata.
    Returns a dict with 'summary' and 'metadata' keys.
    """
    # Step 1: abstractive summarisation
    summary_text, m1 = call_with_retry(
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                   f"Summarise in 3 sentences:\n\n{document[:8000]}"}],
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  # cheaper for summarisation
        max_tokens=150,
    )

    # Step 2: structured extraction — JSON mode via system prompt
    extraction_prompt = f"""Extract from this document.
Return ONLY valid JSON with these keys:
  "title": string or null,
  "date": "YYYY-MM-DD" or null,
  "authors": list of strings,
  "topics": list of strings (max 5),
  "sentiment": "positive" | "neutral" | "negative"

Document: {document[:4000]}"""

    json_text, m2 = call_with_retry(
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": extraction_prompt}],
        system="You are a structured data extractor. Output only valid JSON.",
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
    )

    try:
        metadata = json.loads(json_text)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        # Strip markdown fences if present
        cleaned = json_text.strip().removeprefix("```json").removesuffix("```").strip()
        metadata = json.loads(cleaned)

    total_cost = m1.cost_usd + m2.cost_usd
    print(f"Pipeline cost: ${total_cost:.5f} | latency: {m1.latency_s + m2.latency_s:.2f}s")

    return {"summary": summary_text, "metadata": metadata}


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    sample = """
    Researchers at MIT published findings on 2024-03-15 showing that large language
    models can significantly improve code review quality. Lead authors Jane Smith and
    Bob Jones found that Claude-assisted reviews caught 23% more critical bugs than
    unassisted reviews. The study was broadly positive about AI integration in
    software development workflows.
    """
    result = summarise_and_extract(sample)
    print("Summary:", result["summary"])
    print("Metadata:", json.dumps(result["metadata"], indent=2))
Common Mistakes in LLM Text Applications
Responses truncated mid-sentence or run indefinitely
Always set max_tokens based on expected output length
Use temperature=0.0 for deterministic/factual tasks; 0.7-1.0 for creative tasks
Use RAG for factual tasks; instruct model to cite sources and express uncertainty
Building chat history as string concatenation
Role tokens not respected; model loses conversation structure
Use the messages array format with explicit role fields, not manual text concatenation
Application crashes on rate limits or server errors
Implement exponential backoff retry; catch RateLimitError and APIError separately
Always try/except json.loads(); strip markdown fences; validate schema
Sending entire documents at max context
Slow, expensive, lost-in-the-middle failures on key facts
Chunk documents; retrieve only relevant sections before prompting

# WRONG: string-based conversation history (role tokens not enforced)
history = "User: What is Python?\nAssistant: Python is a language.\nUser: Show me a loop.\n"
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=100,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": history}]  # fragile — roles are just text
)

# RIGHT: structured messages array (roles are API fields, not text)
messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "What is Python?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Python is a high-level programming language."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Show me a loop."},
]
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=100, messages=messages
)
print(resp.content[0].text)
Best Practices for Production Text Systems
Store system prompts and few-shot examples in versioned YAML or Jinja2 templates. Coupling prompts to code makes A/B testing and debugging difficult.
API providers update models without notice. Specify the exact model version (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6 with a specific dated variant) to prevent silent quality regressions.

Track prompt_tokens and completion_tokens per endpoint. Summarisation tasks typically have high prompt/completion ratios; classification tasks have very low completion token counts. Optimise accordingly.
Use smaller models for simple tasks:
A Haiku-class model handling extraction or classification at 10× lower cost frees budget for Sonnet/Opus on complex reasoning tasks. Implement a model tier router.
Always validate structured outputs (JSON schema, regex, custom validators) and retry with an error-correcting prompt on failure rather than propagating malformed data.
Store (prompt, response, model, temperature, tokens, latency, cost) for every production call. This enables debugging, cost analysis, and evaluation dataset construction.
For open-ended tasks where BLEU/ROUGE are uninformative, use a stronger model (GPT-4o or Claude Opus) as an automated judge with a rubric. Monitor judge consistency across prompt versions.

The throughput of LLM text generation is fundamentally limited by GPU memory bandwidth, not compute. Each forward pass reads the entire model's weights from HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), and the KV-cache for the context must also be stored in GPU memory. For a 70B model at BF16 precision, the weights alone consume approximately 140 GB, requiring multiple H100 GPUs. This means latency scales with model size, and horizontal scaling requires careful load balancing at the model server level.

is the primary throughput lever: serving a batch of 32 sequences simultaneously uses roughly the same GPU time as serving 1 sequence (memory bandwidth dominated). Continuous batching (also called in-flight batching) removes the padding overhead of fixed-batch approaches by filling compute slots with new requests as existing sequences complete—used in vLLM, TGI, and TensorRT-LLM.
Cache past key/value tensors to avoid recomputation
Memory usage proportional to sequence length
Fill GPU compute with requests as sequences complete
Small draft model proposes tokens; large model verifies
Requires a fast small model aligned with large model
Reduce weight precision to reduce memory and compute
Cache KV states for repeated prompt prefixes
Up to 90% cost reduction for fixed system prompts

The LLM text generation ecosystem has matured rapidly. At the inference layer, vLLM provides state-of-the-art continuous batching and PagedAttention for GPU-efficient KV-cache management. LiteLLM abstracts across provider APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Cohere, Bedrock) with a unified interface and built-in retry logic. At the application layer, LangChain and LlamaIndex provide higher-level orchestration for RAG pipelines, agents, and multi-step chains.
Native streaming, batch requests, prompt caching
Unified API across 100+ providers, cost tracking
Prompt versioning, A/B testing, quality metrics

# LiteLLM: unified API across providers
from litellm import completion

# Switch between providers by changing the model string
def generate_with_provider(prompt: str, provider: str = "anthropic") -> str:
    models = {
        "anthropic": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
        "openai": "gpt-4o-mini",
        "bedrock": "anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6",
    }
    response = completion(
        model=models[provider],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0.0,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content


# vLLM: self-hosted inference with OpenAI-compatible API
import openai

vllm_client = openai.OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="no-key-needed",  # vLLM doesn't require auth by default
)

def generate_selfhosted(prompt: str, model: str = "meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-instruct") -> str:
    resp = vllm_client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0.0,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.content
Comparison with Alternative Text Generation Approaches
Zero/few-shot, multi-task, no training data needed
Task-specific quality, lower cost at scale
Fast, cheap inference; high classification accuracy
Smaller than LLMs; weaker at open-ended tasks
Cannot handle unseen patterns, brittle maintenance
Cannot synthesise or reason, only retrieves
The decision matrix between these approaches hinges on three factors:

(prototype vs high-volume production). For most new products, start with a prompted LLM to validate the idea, then fine-tune or switch to a cheaper specialised model once the task specification is stable and usage volume justifies training investment.
LLM text systems introduce security risks that have no precedent in traditional software.

is the most significant: if user-supplied text is concatenated into a system prompt without sanitisation, an attacker can inject instructions that override the system's intended behaviour—instructing the model to ignore safety guidelines, exfiltrate context, or produce harmful content. Unlike SQL injection, there is no parameterised query equivalent for natural language; defences are probabilistic, not deterministic.

Never concatenate untrusted user input directly into system prompts. Use structured message roles to separate user content from instructions. Apply semantic classifiers to detect injection attempts in high-risk applications.

If the context window contains sensitive data (PII, credentials, internal documents) and the model can be prompted to repeat or summarise it, a malicious prompt can extract that data. Redact PII before inserting documents into the context.

Adversarial prompts that elicit harmful content from aligned models are a persistent risk. Rely on provider-level safety filters as a first layer and implement your own output classifiers for content moderation at the application layer.

Provider model updates silently change behaviour. Pin model versions and run regression tests on representative prompts after any version change. Monitor output quality metrics in production continuously.

Never trust model outputs as safe for injection into downstream systems. Sanitise generated HTML (XSS), validate generated SQL before execution, and parse JSON with strict schema validation. A model asked to generate code may produce executable payloads.
LLM APIs are expensive; malicious users can exhaust your API budget through prompt flooding. Implement per-user rate limits, token caps, and anomaly detection on usage patterns.
A single autoregressive LLM handles all text tasks (generation, summarisation, translation, QA, classification) through prompting — the task is specified in the input, not the model architecture.

Decoding controls the quality–diversity trade-off: temperature sharpens or flattens the distribution; nucleus sampling (top-p) removes low-probability tail tokens; greedy decoding maximises determinism.
Hallucination is an intrinsic property of next-token prediction. RAG, grounding with citations, and structured output prompts mitigate but do not eliminate it.
Automatic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, perplexity) are unreliable indicators of real-world quality for LLMs. LLM-as-judge evaluation is now common for open-ended tasks.
Prompt engineering (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought) and streaming are the primary tools for controlling LLM text outputs in production.
